2026-04-24T09:15:21.4093991Z Sources: Source F (Chefkoch Wacholdercreme — 7 min per side), Source G (Chefkoch Sardische Art — 7–10 min per side)
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4096603Z Cut profile: Wild boar chops are cut from the back (Rücken) of Sus scrofa. Wild boar meat is darker, leaner and more flavourful than domestic pork, with a pronounced game flavour that pairs well with juniper, gin, and fruit-based sauces. CRITICAL FOOD SAFETY: Wild boar is a known carrier of Trichinella spiralis and in some regions African Swine Fever. It must reach a minimum internal temperature of 71°C throughout the meat — no exceptions. Unlike venison, wild boar must never be served pink. The fat content is lower than domestic pork, meaning chops can dry quickly at high heat; the 7–10 min per side timing in both Chefkoch sources at moderate-high heat is designed to achieve thorough cooking without excessive drying. Grill marks add character.
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4098498Z Pull temp: 71°C minimum, range 68–74°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4099101Z Notes: doneness=well_done, pull_temp_c=71, pull_temp_range_c=[68,74], safety_level=food_safety_critical. NOTE: wild boar must reach 71°C minimum due to Trichinella risk.
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4099523Z

2026-04-24T09:15:21.4109561Z Cut profile: Wild boar shoulder (Schulter) is a well-exercised, collagen-rich cut with bone-in. It needs extended low heat to break down connective tissue — similar to domestic pork shoulder but with a richer, more complex flavour from the animal's varied wild diet. The high-heat start (250°C for 20 min) achieves Maillard browning on the exposed surfaces. The long, slow braise at 120°C ensures Trichinella destruction throughout (71°C must be reached throughout the whole joint) while breaking down collagen. At the probe temperature of 88–95°C the meat will be pull-apart tender. Only 1 verified source for this specific cut — noted below.

The BBC au vin approach uses red wine and chicken stock for 1.5–2 hrs on a low simmer — a classic French method. The Chefkoch German approach uses mustard-coated legs braised with smoked bacon and onions for 45 min, producing a lighter but still rich result. Probe placement: insert into the thickest part of the largest leg (upper thigh), keeping tip clear of the femur. At 82°C the meat will be fully cooked and beginning to relax from the bone.

2026-04-24T09:15:21.4123508Z Cut profile: Oven-roasting rabbit legs at high heat (220°C) creates a golden, lightly crisped exterior while keeping the interior moist. The BBC roast rabbit recipe joints the whole rabbit and roasts all pieces together — legs, saddle, and shoulders. At 220°C/200°C fan, the legs reach safe serving temperature in approximately 35 minutes total (20 min plain + 15 min with aromatics). Unlike braising, oven-roasting produces drier, more concentrated flavour with no braising liquid, relying on the natural moisture of the meat. 2026-04-24T09:15:21.4125974Z ### FILE 11: moose/moose_steak-pan_sear.md
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4126415Z Path: /home/runner/work/meater-in-local-haos/meater-in-local-haos/docs/recipe_research/game/moose/moose_steak-pan_sear.md
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4126910Z Tree: Category=game, folder=moose/, cut_slug=moose_steak, method=pan_sear
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4127336Z Sources: Source L (Chefkoch Elchsteaks — flour, sear, 160°C oven for 2 hrs).
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4128817Z IMPORTANT NOTE: The Chefkoch recipe cooks elk steaks for 2 hours at 160°C which by modern standards vastly overcooks a steak. The recipe reflects older Canadian/North American tradition of long-roasting game. The recommended pull temperature for a proper steak is 57°C (medium-rare), not achieved by 2 hours in a 160°C oven. Include the source faithfully, then note in the MEATER-specific step that modern best practice calls for pulling at 57°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4131666Z Cut profile: Moose (Alces alces) is the largest member of the deer family. Moose steaks — typically cut from the haunch or sirloin — are extremely lean, dark-coloured and deeply flavoured, with a mild gamey character. Like all cervids, moose is best served medium-rare to medium: overcooked moose becomes dry and fibrous. The Chefkoch recipe was submitted from Canada where older game-cooking traditions favoured fully cooked meat; contemporary best practice treats elk/moose steak identically to venison steak (medium-rare at 54–57°C). The honey glaze in Source L adds a caramelised crust that compensates for the lack of surface fat.
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4133321Z Pull temp: 57°C (medium-rare), range 54–60°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4133687Z Notes: doneness=medium_rare, pull_temp_c=57, pull_temp_range_c=[54,60], safety_level=safe_with_rest

2026-04-24T09:15:21.4148066Z Cut profile: Kangaroo (Macropus rufus / M. giganteus) steak is the leanest commercially available red meat with <2% fat in the fillet or rump. It is extremely high in conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and iron. Because of the near-zero fat content, kangaroo must never be cooked past medium (60°C) or it becomes intolerably dry and tough. The 45-minute room-temperature marinade in Source N is essential — cold kangaroo placed in a hot pan will seize immediately on the outside while the centre remains cold. Cook at MODERATE heat (not maximum) for 3 min per side; the cream-pepper sauce added after is both a condiment and a basting medium for the sauce stage. Only 1 verified source for this cut — noted.
2026-04-24T09:15:21.4149859Z Pull temp: 57°C (medium-rare), range 54–60°C.

The two-stage oven method in Source O (4 min sear → 10 min at 140°C → 15 min at 100°C) gently finishes the steak to medium after an initial sear without the drying risk of prolonged high-heat cooking. The cast-iron pan is used without additional oil because the brief prior olive oil rub and the meat's natural surface moisture prevent sticking. Only 1 verified source — noted.

2026-04-24T09:15:21.4171192Z Cut profile: Mutton is sheep over 12 months old (typically 2–3 years or older). Mutton chops — whether rib, loin or shoulder chops — are larger, fattier and more strongly flavoured than lamb chops, with deeper red meat and firm texture from well-exercised muscles. The older the animal, the more pronounced the lanolin-like flavour of the fat, which can be managed by trimming surface fat. Mutton requires higher internal temperatures than lamb to achieve palatable tenderness — the collagen network is denser. The Chefkoch recipe specifies "gar, aber nicht durchbraten" (cooked through but not fully done) — for mutton chops this means reaching ~71°C where the meat is cooked through without being dried out. Only 1 verified source — noted.

2026-04-24T09:15:21.4228609Z Cut profile: Same as FILE 26 but pan context. The 80°C oven finish after the initial sear is a German technique for lean game steaks — it prevents the violent heat of a pan from overshooting the internal temperature after the Maillard crust has formed. Water buffalo entrecôte from Asian or Eastern European farms can be treated identically.

2026-04-24T09:15:21.4235062Z Cut profile: Reindeer (Rangifer tarandus) is the domesticated/semi-domesticated form of caribou, widely consumed in Scandinavia (Norway, Sweden, Finland) and by Indigenous peoples of the Arctic. Reindeer steak is anatomically and gastronomically identical to venison steak from roe deer or red deer — lean, dark crimson, mineral, and gamey in proportion to the animal's diet and age. Scandinavian culinary tradition treats reindeer steak identically to other venison: short, hot pan-sear to medium-rare, served with lingonberry or cloudberry condiments rather than the redcurrant jelly used in the British sources. The BBC GF port sauce and Chefkoch Rehsteak rosa recipes are directly transferable.

Reindeer saddle is particularly prized in Finnish and Norwegian festive cooking. The BBC GF recipe (stuffed with prune-brioche, wrapped in pancetta) represents the British/Continental European approach; in Scandinavia reindeer roast is more commonly served simply with juniper berry sauce and cloudberry jam rather than a stuffing. The pull temperature of 60°C for medium-rare applies equally regardless of accompaniment style.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.7953302Z       ### BISON STEAK SOURCES (3 Chefkoch recipes, all use sear + 80°C oven)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7953582Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7953812Z       **Source 1: Chefkoch — Bisonsteak mit Salicorne**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7954787Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/2812381433245208/Bisonsteak-mit-Salicorne.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7955227Z       Serves: 2 · Total: 50 min · Prep: 10 min · Cook: 40 min
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7955803Z       Ingredients: 2 Bison Entrecôte à 250g, 150g Salicornes (Queller/samphire), 1 small red onion, 1 garlic clove, 2 tbsp oil, pepper
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7956250Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7956520Z       1. Place a flat dish in the oven and preheat to 80°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7956869Z       2. Heat oil in a heavy pan, sear steaks hard on both sides. Transfer to the dish.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7957574Z       3. Sweat Salicornes, onion, and garlic in the pan until coloured. Spread on top of steaks. Steaks need approx. 30 min in the oven (medium/rare).
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7958034Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7958285Z       **Source 2: Chefkoch — Bisonsteak auf Paprika**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7958653Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/2841491436191027/Bisonsteak-auf-Paprika.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7959078Z       Serves: 2 · Total: 55 min · Prep: 25 min · Cook: 30 min
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7959888Z       Ingredients: 2 Bison Entrecôte à 250g, 1 red bell pepper, 1 small red spring onion chopped, 1 red chilli sliced, 3cm ginger finely chopped, 2 tsp Tasmanian Leatherwood honey, 4 tbsp oil, 1 tsp Maggi, 1 tsp pepper, salt
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7960527Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7960741Z       1. Preheat oven to 80°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7961039Z       2. Dice bell pepper, slice chilli into rings, finely chop onion and ginger.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7961861Z       3. Heat 2 tbsp oil in a pan, sweat vegetables. Season with honey, pepper, Maggi. Transfer to a flat baking dish and place in the oven. Heat remaining oil in a heavy pan and sear steaks on both sides. Place steaks on the vegetable bed and cook 25 min (medium) in the oven.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7962782Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7963026Z       **Source 3: Chefkoch — Bisonsteak in Rosmarin**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7963385Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/2841471436190764/Bisonsteak-in-Rosmarin.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7963812Z       Serves: 1 · Total: 45 min · Prep: 15 min · Cook: 30 min
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7964493Z       Ingredients: 250g Bison Entrecôte, 2 sprigs rosemary, 5 tbsp Robusta olive oil
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7964816Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7965152Z       1. Preheat oven to 80°C (top/bottom heat). Strip rosemary needles and roughly chop.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7965824Z       2. Place 4 tbsp olive oil and rosemary in a flat ovenproof dish, put in preheated oven. Heat 1 tbsp oil in a heavy pan and sear entrecôte hard on both sides.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7966444Z       3. Place meat in the preheated dish, turn in the oil. Cook 25 min (medium) in the oven.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.7967718Z       **Source A: BBC Good Food — Venison steak with Port sauce**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7968071Z       URL: https://www.bbcgoodfood.com/recipes/venison-steak-port-sauce
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7968455Z       Serves: 4 · Recipe from Good Food magazine, October 2010
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7969209Z       Ingredients: 750g small potatoes (halved/quartered), 2 tbsp olive oil, 4 venison steaks, 1 tbsp cracked black pepper, chopped parsley to serve. For sauce: zest 1 orange (in strips) + its juice, 6 tbsp redcurrant jelly, 4 tbsp port, 1 cinnamon stick
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7977055Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7977584Z       1. Make the gravy by simmering all sauce ingredients together until redcurrant jelly has melted. Keep warm.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7978204Z       2. Steam or simmer potatoes until just tender, about 8 mins, then drain and add a few drops of oil.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7979248Z       3. Lay venison on a board. Sprinkle black pepper and a little salt on each side, pressing pepper into the steaks. Heat remaining oil in a pan. When shimmering, add steaks 2 at a time. Cook 2 min per side for rare, 3 min for medium, 4 min for well done. Return all to pan, pour over sauce, warm 1 min, sprinkle with parsley. Serve with potatoes.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7980116Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7980467Z       **Source B: BBC Good Food — Pan-fried venison with blackberry sauce**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7980897Z       URL: https://www.bbcgoodfood.com/recipes/pan-fried-venison-blackberry-sauce
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7981335Z       Serves: 2 · Recipe from Good Food magazine, November 2007
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7982067Z       Ingredients: 1 tbsp olive oil, 2 thick venison steaks or 4 medallions, 1 tbsp balsamic vinegar, 150ml beef stock (made with 2 tsp Knorr Touch of Taste), 2 tbsp redcurrant jelly, 1 garlic clove (crushed), 85g fresh or frozen blackberries
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7982729Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7983396Z       1. Heat oil in a frying pan, cook venison 5 min on first side, then turn and cook 3–5 min more (depending on how rare you like it; 5–6 min each side for well done). Lift meat from pan and set aside to rest.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7984550Z       2. Add balsamic vinegar to pan, pour in stock, redcurrant jelly and garlic. Stir over high heat to blend, then add blackberries and cook until they soften. Serve with the venison.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7985107Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7985352Z       **Source C: Chefkoch — Rehsteak rosa gebraten**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7985715Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/3954921606145264/Rehsteak-rosa-gebraten.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7986234Z       Serves: 1 · Total: 13 min · Prep: 4 min · Cook: 4 min · Rest: 5 min · Rating: 5/5 (3 ratings)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7986662Z       Subtitle: "Auf die einfache Art, ohne Backofen" (Simple way, without oven)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7987144Z       Ingredients: 1 venison steak (roe deer) approx. 1.5cm thick, 1 tbsp sunflower oil, 1 tbsp butter, salt and pepper
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7987528Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7987831Z       1. Briefly rinse meat cold, pat dry, leave at room temperature for about 1 hour before frying.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7989006Z       2. In a pan, heat sunflower oil with butter until it stops foaming. Fry venison steak on each side for just under 2 minutes. Fold a large piece of aluminium foil double and wrap the steak inside. Rest on the counter for 5 minutes, then season with pepper and salt.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7990159Z       3. Optional: deglaze pan with a little red wine or bouillon and bind with ice-cold butter or Mondamin (starch). Note: "This method is safe and quick for any beginner who does not want to over-cook their venison steak."
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7990789Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7991113Z       **Source D: Chefkoch — Reh- oder Hirschsteaks mit verschiedenen Marinaden**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7991622Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/1520541257290915/Reh-oder-Hirschsteaks-mit-verschiedenen-Marinaden.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7992214Z       Serves: 4 · Total: 24 hrs 30 min (incl. overnight marination) · Rating: 4.5/5 (6 ratings)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7992673Z       Ingredients: 1 kg steaks from roe deer or red deer haunch, cut 2–3cm thick.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7993255Z       Marinade 1 (Chili-Honey): several chillies chopped, 10 tbsp pumpkin seed oil, 10 tbsp honey, salt and pepper, some parsley chopped, 4 garlic cloves chopped
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7994078Z       Marinade 2 (Wild-Herb): 10 tbsp olive oil, some bay leaves, juniper berries, rosemary, thyme, salt and pepper, 4 garlic cloves chopped
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7994995Z       Marinade 3 (Beer-Mustard): 1 bottle dark beer, 10 tbsp olive oil, 2 tbsp Dijon mustard, 1 small onion finely chopped, some bay leaves, juniper berries, 4 garlic cloves chopped, salt and pepper, some basil
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7995579Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7995882Z       1. Cut a roe deer or red deer haunch into steaks approx. 2–3cm thick.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7996314Z       2. Prepare chosen marinade from the ingredients above, marinate steaks overnight in the fridge.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7996827Z       3. Grill the steaks — they must not be fully cooked through so they remain juicier.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7997167Z       4. They can also be pan-fried.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7997562Z       Tip: "Wild boar is also excellent for grilling. However, it is recommended to cook wild boar fully through."
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7997938Z

2026-04-24T09:15:45.7998648Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7998955Z       **Source E: BBC Good Food — Stuffed saddle of venison with prunes & brioche**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7999380Z       URL: https://www.bbcgoodfood.com/recipes/stuffed-saddle-venison-prunes-brioche
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7999740Z       Recipe from Good Food magazine, December 2017
2026-04-24T09:15:45.7999988Z       Method (key points):
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8000788Z       1. Heat oven to 180°C/160°C fan/gas 4. Make stuffing: melt butter, fry onion, garlic, prunes and bacon until onion translucent and bacon slightly coloured. Add brioche, cook until combined and broken down. Add sage, season, remove from heat.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8001645Z       2. Lay pancetta on parchment+foil. Lay venison saddle on top skin-side down, spoon stuffing down the centre.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8002172Z       3. Fold in sides of venison with pancetta, tie with strings, enclose in foil and parchment parcel.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8003128Z       4. Roast on baking sheet for 40–50 min or until meat thermometer reaches 60°C, for medium rare. Rest 10 min, remove foil/parchment, brush all over with butter, return to oven for final 10 min. Leave to rest 10–15 min before removing string and slicing.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8004417Z       ### WILD BOAR CHOP SOURCES
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8004656Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8004929Z       **Source F: Chefkoch — Wildschweinkoteletts mit Wacholdercreme**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8005377Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/2049741331587566/Wildschweinkoteletts-mit-Wacholdercreme.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8005894Z       Serves: 4 · Total: 45 min · Prep: 20 min · Cook: 25 min · Rating: 4/5 (1 rating)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8006624Z       Ingredients: 750g wild boar back with bone (4 chops), salt, pepper, oil, 8 juniper berries, 4cl gin, 250ml game stock (Wildfond), 20g ice-cold butter, 125ml whipped cream
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8007152Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8007484Z       1. Cut wild boar back into chops. Season with salt and pepper from the mill, sear in hot oil on both sides.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8008046Z       2. Crush 8 juniper berries and add to the meat. Fry chops on each side approx. 7 minutes, then remove from pan and keep warm.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8009230Z       3. Deglaze pan residues with a splash of gin and fill up with 250ml game stock. (Leave juniper berries in sauce or strain — depending on preference.) Reduce sauce slightly, bind with ice-cold butter, fold in whipped cream.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8010259Z       4. Serve chops with the sauce.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8010563Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8010972Z       **Source G: Chefkoch — Wildschweinkoteletts auf sardische Art**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8011657Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/656531167378180/Wildschweinkoteletts-auf-sardische-Art.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8012486Z       Serves: 4 · Total: 40 min · Rating: 3/5 (3 ratings)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8012863Z       Subtitle: Sardinian agrodolce style
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8013598Z       Ingredients: 60g dried prunes (stoned), 60g raisins, 4 wild boar chops from the back, 50g lean bacon, 3 tbsp oil, 40g dark chocolate, 25g sugar, 4 bay leaves, 50ml red wine vinegar, 50ml Italian red wine, salt, pepper, pinch ground cinnamon
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8014333Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8014550Z       1. Soak prunes and raisins in warm water. Drain well.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8015788Z       2. Rinse meat, pat dry, season both sides with salt and pepper. Finely dice bacon, render in heated oil, then fry chops on each side approx. 7–10 minutes. Meanwhile grate chocolate and heat with sugar, bay leaf, red wine vinegar and red wine, stirring. Add drained prunes and raisins, season with a pinch of cinnamon, simmer gently 3 minutes. Season sauce with salt and pepper.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8017024Z       3. To serve: pour sauce with prunes and raisins over chops, garnish with bay leaves, serve on a warmed platter. Serve with dumplings and mixed salad.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8018441Z       **Source H: Chefkoch — Wildschweinschulter ganz einfach**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8018851Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/3444381513162705/Wildschweinschulter-ganz-einfach.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8019373Z       Serves: 3 · Total: 3 hrs 55 min · Prep: 35 min · Cook: 3 hrs 20 min · Rating: 5/5 (5 ratings)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8019769Z       Subtitle: "Geschmort ohne Schnickschnack" (Braised without fuss)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8020727Z       Ingredients: 1.5kg wild boar shoulder with bone, 250ml dry red wine (Merlot), 400ml game stock (Wildfond), 1 bunch soup vegetables (Suppengemüse), 1 tbsp tomato paste, 2 juniper berries, 2 small bay leaves, 2 onions (if not in the vegetable bunch), salt and pepper
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8021446Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8021658Z       1. Preheat oven to 250°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8022274Z       2. Rub shoulder with oil and salt. Clean and roughly chop vegetables. Mix vegetables with tomato paste in a roasting tin, place shoulder on top, roast approx. 20 min until shoulder and vegetables get some colour.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8023225Z       3. Add remaining ingredients (wine, stock, juniper berries, bay leaves), put lid on. Reduce oven to 120°C and let everything simmer for approx. 3 hrs.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8024043Z       4. The shoulder can rest in the turned-off oven while making the sauce (pass vegetables through a sieve, reduce or thicken sauce, season). Serve with fried Chinese cabbage and mashed potato.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8025319Z       ### RABBIT SOURCES
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8025509Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8025740Z       **Source I: BBC Good Food — Rabbit au vin**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8026040Z       URL: https://www.bbcgoodfood.com/recipes/rabbit-au-vin
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8026407Z       Serves: 6 · Recipe from Good Food magazine, November 2014
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8027587Z       Ingredients: 450g shallots, 2 onions (thinly sliced), 2 celery sticks (thinly sliced), 300g carrots (thickly sliced), 200g pack unsmoked bacon lardons, 3 wild rabbits jointed into shoulders, legs and saddle pieces (600g each gutted weight), 2 tbsp plain flour (seasoned), 25g butter + a knob, 1 tbsp olive oil, 600ml full-bodied red wine, 400ml chicken stock, 2 bay leaves, few thyme sprigs + extra to garnish, 300g button mushrooms
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8028682Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8029297Z       1. Soak shallots in boiling water 5 min, drain, peel. Slice other vegetables. Heat a large wide flameproof casserole, add lardons. Fry 5 min until fat runs, add vegetables, cook 10 min until golden and softening. Tip into a bowl.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8030514Z       2. Toss rabbit with flour, tap off excess. Heat half butter+oil in dish, brown half the rabbit 10 min until golden all over. Add splash of water, scrape dish, tip juices into a jug. Repeat with second batch, deglazing with wine (reduce by about a third).
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8032000Z       3. Add rabbit and vegetables to wine, pour in stock, stir in herbs, bring to a simmer. Cover (leaving small gap for steam), simmer 1.5–2 hrs until meat falls from bones. Thicken sauce if desired. Finish: heat knob butter in frying pan, cook mushrooms with salt, pepper, thyme over high heat until browned. Spoon over stew.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8032830Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8033075Z       **Source J: Chefkoch — Geschmorte Kaninchenkeulen**
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8033447Z       URL: https://www.chefkoch.de/rezepte/1565741264063511/Geschmorte-Kaninchenkeulen.html
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8034034Z       Serves: 4 · Total: 12 hrs 15 min (incl. overnight rest) · Prep: 15 min · Cook: 45 min · Rating: 4.7/5 (17 ratings)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8034662Z       Ingredients: 4 rabbit legs, 125g smoked bacon, 2 onions, salt and pepper, mustard, oil, sauce thickener (optional)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8035037Z       Method:
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8035397Z       1. Brush rabbit legs all over with mustard, season well with salt and pepper, leave overnight to rest (best overnight).
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8036409Z       2. Heat oil in a pot until hot, brown legs all over. Meanwhile peel onions and cut into rough pieces. Cut smoked bacon into rough pieces. Once legs are well browned, lay onions and bacon on top of the meat. Cover the pot and braise approx. 45 minutes, turning occasionally and adding a little water as needed.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8037595Z       3. When meat is tender, remove meat and bacon and keep warm. Blend the braising juices with a hand blender. Thicken with sauce thickener if desired. Serve with salt potatoes, dumplings and red cabbage.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8112340Z       ### FILE 1: bison/bison_steak-pan_sear.md
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8112792Z       Path: /home/runner/work/meater-in-local-haos/meater-in-local-haos/docs/recipe_research/game/bison/bison_steak-pan_sear.md
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8113306Z       Tree: Category=game, folder=bison/, cut_slug=bison_steak, method=pan_sear
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8113655Z       Sources: Sources 1, 2, 3 (3 Chefkoch bison recipes). 
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8115513Z       Cut profile: Bison (Bison bison) entrecôte/ribeye is a lean, iron-rich muscle from the rib section. Bison carries ~40% less fat than beef, making it extremely easy to overcook. The 80°C oven method used by all three Chefkoch authors gives gentle, even heat after the initial sear, preventing the outer layers from drying before the centre reaches temperature. Because bison is so lean, probe placement must be central to avoid reading the hotter surface. Similar to wagyu beef in that it rewards a short, hard sear + gentle finish.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8117072Z       Pull temp: 60°C (medium-rare), range 57–63°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8117483Z       Notes for coding: doneness=medium_rare, pull_temp_c=60, pull_temp_range_c=[57,63], safety_level=safe_with_rest
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8117856Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8118055Z       ### FILE 2: venison/venison_steak-pan_sear.md
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8118517Z       Path: /home/runner/work/meater-in-local-haos/meater-in-local-haos/docs/recipe_research/game/venison/venison_steak-pan_sear.md
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8119042Z       Tree: Category=game, folder=venison/, cut_slug=venison_steak, method=pan_sear
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8119509Z       Sources: Source A (BBC GF port sauce), Source B (BBC GF blackberry sauce), Source C (Chefkoch Rehsteak rosa)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8121791Z       Cut profile: Venison steak most commonly refers to a haunch (leg) steak from roe deer (Reh) or red deer (Hirsch). Venison is exceptionally lean — leaner even than bison — with a deep crimson colour and a mineral, gamey flavour that is accentuated by overcooking. The optimal texture is achieved at medium-rare (55–57°C), where the centre is still pink but the connective tissue of the outer muscle has relaxed. The short cooking time (2–5 min per side) means probe placement is critical: insert parallel to the surface and lengthways into the thickest part to get a true centre reading. Unlike beef, venison does not benefit from extended aging past ~7 days; fresh venison should be treated quickly at high heat.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8123588Z       Pull temp: 57°C (medium-rare), range 54–60°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8123970Z       Notes: doneness=medium_rare, pull_temp_c=57, pull_temp_range_c=[54,60], safety_level=safe_with_rest
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8124412Z       
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8124609Z       ### FILE 3: venison/venison_steak-grill.md
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8125080Z       Path: /home/runner/work/meater-in-local-haos/meater-in-local-haos/docs/recipe_research/game/venison/venison_steak-grill.md
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8125589Z       Tree: Category=game, folder=venison/, cut_slug=venison_steak, method=grill
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8126332Z       Sources: Source D (Chefkoch Reh/Hirsch mit Marinaden — primary grill source), Source A (BBC GF port sauce, adapts to grill), Source C (Chefkoch Rehsteak rosa — notes it can be pan-fried)
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8128454Z       Cut profile: Same cut as venison_steak-pan_sear (haunch steak). Grilling adds Maillard crust from direct high heat and smoky flavour that complements the gamey notes of wild deer. The three marinade options from Chefkoch (Chili-Honey, Wild-Herb, Beer-Mustard) all serve to add moisture to the lean meat and infuse flavour during the overnight rest. The tip "must not be fully cooked through" is universal in German game cooking for venison — it should remain pink (rosa) in the centre. Direct heat requires extra attention to pulling at the right moment.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8129922Z       Pull temp: 57°C, range 54–60°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8130279Z       Notes: doneness=medium_rare, pull_temp_c=57, pull_temp_range_c=[54,60], safety_level=safe_with_rest

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8131273Z       Path: /home/runner/work/meater-in-local-haos/meater-in-local-haos/docs/recipe_research/game/venison/venison_loin-pan_sear.md

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8132255Z       Sources: Same sources as venison_steak (A, B, C) but note that loin/backstrap/tenderloin is even more delicate.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8134594Z       Cut profile: Venison loin refers to the backstrap (longissimus dorsi) running along the spine, or the tenderloin (psoas minor) which lies inside the pelvic cavity. Both are the most prized cuts on a deer carcass. The loin is ultra-lean, tender without connective tissue, and has virtually zero fat marbling. It cooks faster than a haunch steak of equivalent thickness — a 3cm loin medallion needs only 90 seconds per side on a very hot pan. The loin is particularly unforgiving: past 60°C it begins to toughen quickly. Probe insertion for a loin medallion: insert horizontally through the side, keeping the tip dead-centre. For a whole backstrap roll: insert lengthways from the thick end.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8141817Z       Cut profile: Venison roast — typically the saddle (both loins on the bone) or haunch (bone-in leg) — is the showpiece cut for formal game dinners. The saddle is extremely tender and lean; the haunch contains more connective tissue but both reward medium-rare service. Pancetta or bacon wrapping (as in the BBC GF recipe) provides basting fat that the lean venison lacks. The stuffing in the BBC recipe adds moisture and acts as insulation to slow cooking, meaning the probe temperature is the definitive doneness guide. Rest time is as important as cook time for a large roast: 10–15 min allows the outer crust to cool slightly and redistribution of juices. Probe must be inserted in the deepest part of the meat mass, away from bone and stuffing.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8145163Z       Path: /home/runner/work/meater-in-local-haos/meater-in-local-haos/docs/recipe_research/game/wild_boar/wild_boar_chop-grill.md

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8148780Z       Cut profile: Wild boar chops are cut from the back (Rücken) of Sus scrofa. Wild boar meat is darker, leaner and more flavourful than domestic pork, with a pronounced game flavour that pairs well with juniper, gin, and fruit-based sauces. CRITICAL FOOD SAFETY: Wild boar is a known carrier of Trichinella spiralis and in some regions African Swine Fever. It must reach a minimum internal temperature of 71°C throughout the meat — no exceptions. Unlike venison, wild boar must never be served pink. The fat content is lower than domestic pork, meaning chops can dry quickly at high heat; the 7–10 min per side timing in both Chefkoch sources at moderate-high heat is designed to achieve thorough cooking without excessive drying. Grill marks add character.

At the probe temperature of 88–95°C the meat will be pull-apart tender. Only 1 verified source for this specific cut — noted below.

The recipe reflects older Canadian/North American tradition of long-roasting game. The recommended pull temperature for a proper steak is 57°C (medium-rare), not achieved by 2 hours in a 160°C oven. Include the source faithfully, then note in the MEATER-specific step that modern best practice calls for pulling at 57°C.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8184458Z       Cut profile: Moose (Alces alces) is the largest member of the deer family. Moose steaks — typically cut from the haunch or sirloin — are extremely lean, dark-coloured and deeply flavoured, with a mild gamey character. Like all cervids, moose is best served medium-rare to medium: overcooked moose becomes dry and fibrous. The Chefkoch recipe was submitted from Canada where older game-cooking traditions favoured fully cooked meat; contemporary best practice treats elk/moose steak identically to venison steak (medium-rare at 54–57°C). The honey glaze in Source L adds a caramelised crust that compensates for the lack of surface fat.

The 45-minute room-temperature marinade in Source N is essential — cold kangaroo placed in a hot pan will seize immediately on the outside while the centre remains cold. Cook at MODERATE heat (not maximum) for 3 min per side; the cream-pepper sauce added after is both a condiment and a basting medium for the sauce stage. Only 1 verified source for this cut — noted.

The two-stage oven method in Source O (4 min sear → 10 min at 140°C → 15 min at 100°C) gently finishes the steak to medium after an initial sear without the drying risk of prolonged high-heat cooking. The cast-iron pan is used without additional oil because the brief prior olive oil rub and the meat's natural surface moisture prevent sticking. Only 1 verified source — noted.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8225264Z       Cut profile: Mutton is sheep over 12 months old (typically 2–3 years or older). Mutton chops — whether rib, loin or shoulder chops — are larger, fattier and more strongly flavoured than lamb chops, with deeper red meat and firm texture from well-exercised muscles. The older the animal, the more pronounced the lanolin-like flavour of the fat, which can be managed by trimming surface fat. Mutton requires higher internal temperatures than lamb to achieve palatable tenderness — the collagen network is denser. The Chefkoch recipe specifies "gar, aber nicht durchbraten" (cooked through but not fully done) — for mutton chops this means reaching ~71°C where the meat is cooked through without being dried out. Only 1 verified source — noted.
2026-04-24T09:15:45.8227257Z       Pull temp: 71°C, range 68–77°C.

A medium-rare mutton leg (77°C) is pink-grey throughout with relaxed fibres. The Mediterranean wet garlic rub (15 cloves, oregano, mint, paprika, olive oil, lemon) is equally effective on mutton, though the flavour is more robust. Unlike lamb, mutton benefits from longer oven time to develop flavour; a 2-kg bone-in mutton leg may need 3–3.5 hrs at 163°C.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8244317Z       Cut profile: Mutton shoulder is rich in collagen-bound connective tissue and intermuscular fat that renders during long oven cooking. The shoulder is the ideal cut for mutton slow-roasting because the fat cap bastes the meat throughout cooking. The Chefkoch recipe uses the signature trick of piercing the foil seal 3–4 times — this allows some moisture to escape while retaining the steam environment needed for collagen conversion. Fall-apart mutton shoulder needs to exceed 80°C and ideally rest at 82–88°C for 30+ minutes.

2026-04-24T09:15:45.8276884Z       Cut profile: Water buffalo (Bubalus bubalis) steak, while less common than American bison steak in European markets, shares the same lean, iron-rich muscle structure. The bison entrecôte recipes from Chefkoch (Sources 1 and 2) are the best available proxy: the same sear-plus-gentle-finish principle applies. On a grill: sear 2–3 min per side over direct heat, then move to indirect zone to reach 60°C internal. The salicorne (samphire) garnish in Source 1 adds a briny note that works well with the mineral flavour of lean red meat.

Scandinavian culinary tradition treats reindeer steak identically to other venison: short, hot pan-sear to medium-rare, served with lingonberry or cloudberry condiments rather than the redcurrant jelly used in the British sources. 2026-04-24T09:16:19.1281024Z

2026-04-24T09:16:19.1283242Z       4. Translate non-English recipes if needed (you can translate inline).
2026-04-24T09:16:19.1283844Z       5. Write the filled file content using `edit` (replace the stub content).
